steps.title: Identify the urgent direct address
steps.content: The claim requires Wheatley to directly implore—that is, urgently plead with—Virtue itself. Look for the quotation where she addresses Virtue directly and uses commanding language to request aid, rather than discussing or contemplating Virtue from a distance.

steps.title: Match the active request for assistance
steps.content:
Choice A — "Attend me, Virtue, thro' my youthful years! / O leave me not to the false joys of time! / But guide my steps to endless life and bliss." directly invokes “Attend me, Virtue” and employs urgent commands—”leave me not,” “guide my steps”—requesting Virtue's aid toward a specific future goal: “endless life and bliss.” The other choices show the speaker either withdrawing from Virtue's mystery, attempting intellectual comprehension of it, or receiving its comfort—none depict the direct urgent plea for aid the claim describes.

That matches Choice A — "Attend me, Virtue, thro' my youthful years! / O leave me not to the false joys of time! / But guide my steps to endless life and bliss.".
